appeal_letter:
Dear UnitedHealthcare Appeals Review,

I am writing to appeal the denial of an Eating Disorder Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP) for [Member Name], dated [date]. The denial cites that the patient is at 88% of ideal body weight with stable vital signs, falling outside the numerical thresholds in UHC's guidelines. This appeal asks you to review the clinical trajectory, which shows the patient is in active and accelerating decline that the current level of care cannot arrest.

The patient's measured weight of 88% IBW reflects a current snapshot, not clinical stability. The treating provider documents that the patient is actively restricting food intake and losing 2 to 3 pounds per week. At this rate of decline, the patient will fall below the 85% IBW threshold within a matter of weeks, at which point the clinical situation will be substantially more acute. The appropriate standard is not whether the patient has crossed a numeric threshold today, but whether the current treatment intensity can prevent that crossing — and the record shows it cannot.

Moreover, the apparent weight stability has been maintained only through significant external intervention at home. A patient whose weight is sustained only by external pressure, and who is nonetheless losing ground, is not clinically stable in any meaningful sense. UHC's guidelines speak to the intent of the criteria, and that intent is to ensure patients with active eating disorders receive the appropriate level of care before a medical crisis.

The treating physician and the IOP program both assess this patient as meeting criteria for the higher level of care. We ask that the denial be reconsidered in light of the patient's documented trajectory.

We request approval of the IOP level of care. Please confirm receipt and the deadline for any further submission.

This letter is a draft for your review.

feature_scores.evidence: Clinical trajectory argument is strong but relies on general framing rather than quoting specific UHC guideline language.
Correctly shifts from threshold-based to trajectory-based clinical reasoning.
The 2-3 lb/week decline figure is specific and directly challenges the 'stable' characterization.

gaps: moderate: grounding (anchor 3) — quoting UHC's guideline intent language would sharpen the argument
outcome.critique: Good trajectory-based framing that reframes 'stable' as externally maintained and actively declining.
rationale: Weight trajectory rather than point-in-time threshold is the right clinical argument for a declining patient.
The external-intervention framing (force-feeding) challenges the 'stable vital signs' characterization.

risk_flags: UHC may apply the guideline thresholds strictly; an independent clinical review with a strong provider declaration is advisable.

mirror.what_was_denied: An Eating Disorder Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP).
mirror.why_they_said_no: They say the patient is at 88% of ideal body weight with stable vital signs, which does not meet the thresholds for IOP.
mirror.deadline_note: Check your denial letter for the appeal filing deadline and submit before that date.
mirror.strongest_angle: The patient is losing 2 to 3 pounds a week — the number looks stable today but is actively falling, and the current level of care is not stopping that.

citations_used.title: Denial letter
citations_used.quote: currently at 88% of your ideal body weight (IBW) and your vital signs are stable without severe bradycardia

missing_evidence_checklist: Weekly weight log documenting the rate of decline
Provider's note explaining that apparent stability is maintained only through external intervention
IOP program's clinical assessment letter supporting the higher level of care
